Items.Title: 你为什么还不在 Neovim 上？ | Why aren't you on Neovim yet?
Items.Description:
塞缪尔·福尔

发表于今天才发现 Neovim。 我不敢相信我错过了多少！

  • 与 VIM 完全一样，但速度更快
  • 更好的表现
  • 与我广泛的 VIM 配置一起开箱即用
  • 安装和配置轻而易举，无需更改任何内容
  • 我有没有提到它更快？

显然它是免费和开源的。

如果您想开始，这里有一个快速安装指南：只需将其复制粘贴到~/.config/nvim/init.vim

 set runtimepath^=~/.vim runtimepath+=~/.vim/after let &packpath = &runtimepath source ~/.vimrc

这将使 Neovim 获取您当前的 VIM 配置。 所以如果你最终因为某种原因不喜欢 Neovim，你的 VIM 配置仍然完好无损。

那么，您为什么还不使用 Neovim？



原文:

Samuel FAURE

Posted on May 11

Just discovered Neovim today. I can't believe how much I've been missing out!

  • Exactly like VIM, but faster
  • Better performances
  • Works out-of-the-box with my extensive VIM configuration
  • Installation and configuration was a breeze, don't need to change anything
  • Did I mention that it was faster?

And obviously it's free and open-source.

Here's a quick installation guide if you want to get started: just copy-paste this in ~/.config/nvim/init.vim

set runtimepath^=~/.vim runtimepath+=~/.vim/after
let &packpath = &runtimepath
source ~/.vimrc

This will make Neovim pick up your current VIM config. So if you end up not liking Neovim for some reason, your VIM config is still intact.

So, why aren't you on Neovim yet?
Items.Author: samuelfaure

Items.Title: 什么是 Jamstack？ | What is Jamstack?
Items.Description:
我被问到的一个常见问题是“什么是 Jamstack？” 我已经与 Jamstack 合作了五年多，我想借此机会揭开这个技术堆栈的神秘面纱，并向您保证 Jamstack 与吐司和黄油完全无关（尽管我们希望这样做）。 在这篇文章中，我们将探讨 Jamstack 是什么、为什么 Jamstack 进入 Web 开发领域、“Jamstack-adjacent”技术，以及如何开始在 Jamstack 上构建。

如果你来这里是为了 TL;DR，我得到了你。

它不是一组框架或服务，也不是与任何特定品牌或技术堆栈相关的。 它是出于解决一些非常有趣的问题的需要而发展起来的。

关于 Jamstack 中的 JAM

那时，Jamstack 意味着结合使用 JavaScript、API 和标记来构建静态网站——这些网站被打包并作为静态 HTML 文件和来自 CDN 的资产提供服务。 这些静态站点可能作为 HTML 文件的集合存在于代码存储库中，也可能使用静态站点生成器或构建脚本生成，其中一组指令作为代码将模板代码转换为静态 HTML 文件并将它们存储在 CDN 上.

此后，它移至Jamstack以反映其向架构方法的演变，而不是描述可能使用的技术。 您不一定必须使用 API（甚至 JavaScript）来构建 Jamstack 站点！

这就是所涵盖的术语，但 Jamstack 在 2015 年出现时有什么不同？

传统上，对网页的请求由托管服务器处理（稍后会详细介绍），该服务器在将 HTML 文档发送回用户浏览器之前实时构建 HTML 文档。 如果网页大而复杂，并且页面请求远离源服务器，这意味着网站可能会很慢，从而给最终用户带来不好的体验。 随着越来越多的人开始将网络用于一切，快速体验是使网站可用、可访问的关键——尤其是在互联网连接速度较慢、进行销售、分发信息和发展业务的情况下。

首先，Jamstack 站点以预先生成和存储网站页面为中心——这意味着服务器不需要在页面请求时构建任何内容。 其次，这些预先生成的静态资产由内容交付网络 (CDN) 提供服务——CDN 是一个分布在世界各地的服务器网络，它们协同工作，从可能的最近服务器位置向用户提供缓存的内容。 这保证了比固定服务器位置的传统请求、构建和服务模型更快的体验。 在 Jamstack 之前，网站是使用完整的全栈框架构建的，包括后端服务器代码、前端模板、数据库——一切。 新产品功能的构建、测试和发布到生产可能需要数周时间，软件升级可能存在风险且难以回滚，开发环境也难以配置。 这意味着后端 API 服务、前端模板、数据库和所有其他服务被分离和简化，允许不同学科的开发人员相互并行构建、测试和发布，并且速度更快。

在构建过程中，Jamstack 站点可以调用任意数量的外部 API 服务来获取数据以预生成静态页面。 一旦构建了 Jamstack 站点，它就会被缓存为不可变的部署——一组无法更改的静态文件。 这意味着如果需要，可以在部署之间快速轻松地回滚和转发生产中的 Jamstack 站点。 更重要的是，一个中断的后端 API 更改不会对静态 Jamstack 站点产生任何影响，直到下次重建站点时，使 Jamstack 站点在部署到生产环境后更加稳定且不易出现错误。 从经验来看，这种解耦的方法还使前端开发人员能够完全拥有他们的代码、流程和实践，而不受庞大的单片全栈可怕代码库的摆布。

在大规模单体应用和请求-构建-服务模型的时代，开发和 IT 运营团队负责管理他们自己的服务器（因此，上面的“托管服务器”）。 这带来了许多挑战，包括管理安全和软件升级、DNS (!)，以及在网站流量增加或减少时向上或向下扩展服务器的可用性。 这也大大降低了建立网站并让全世界享受的门槛。 使用 Jamstack，即使是初学者也可以在几分钟内建立一个网站。 编写静态 HTML 文件，放到 CDN 上，自动获取 URL 和 SSL 证书，分享给朋友。

相邻的 Jamstack 技术

请记住——Jamstack 是一种架构工作方式。 您可以选择使用无服务器功能、边缘功能、内容管理系统、其他 API 服务等来增强和丰富您的 Jamstack 站点——但 Jamstack 的核心是没有托管服务器的静态优先方法。

如何开始您的第一个 Jamstack 网站

即用型资产

  • 在没有托管服务器的情况下提供的任何交互
  • 由 JavaScript 和通过 API 解耦的服务的某种组合提供的任何交互性

    • 如果您是 Jamstack 的新手，我个人不建议您立即开始使用静态站点生成器。 相反，我建议从尽可能小的开始。 提供单个 HTML 文件（即用型资产）

  • 来自Netlify等云托管服务提供商（无托管服务器）
  • 调用客户端上的外部 API 以完成某些事情（如果需要）

    • 在需要时添加额外的页面，可能会添加静态站点生成器或构建工具以在构建时生成动态页面。 需要时添加额外功能； 也许你会使用类似的技术，比如无服务器功能——如果合适的话。

    因此，选择您的项目，选择您的云托管服务提供商，从小处着手，享受从 CDN 提供静态文件的乐趣，然后享受旅程。



    原文:

    A common question I get asked is “What is Jamstack?” I’ve been working with Jamstack solidly for over five years now, and I wanted to take an opportunity to demystify this technology stack, and reassure you that Jamstack has absolutely nothing to do with toast and butter (even though we wish it did). In this post, we’ll explore what Jamstack is, why Jamstack entered the web dev scene, “Jamstack-adjacent” technologies, and how you can get started building on the Jamstack.

    If you’re here for the TL;DR, I got you.

    A Jamstack definition

    Jamstack is an architectural model centred on serving pre-generated, highly portable static assets from cloud hosting platforms, usually via a Content Delivery Network (CDN). Jamstack architecture is elevated and enhanced by a wide variety of modern adjacent development tooling such as serverless and edge functions, content management systems, version control integration, and API services.

    Jamstack is a way of working. It’s not a group of frameworks or services or tied to any particular brands or tech stack. Jamstack is defined by how you build websites, rather than the tools with which you choose to build them. And it evolved out of the need to solve some very interesting problems.

    About the JAM in Jamstack

    The term “Jamstack” has been around since 2015, and came from the brains of Matt Biilmann and Chris Bach, founders of web developer cloud hosting platform Netlify. Back then, Jamstack meant using a combination of JavaScript, APIs, and Markup together to build static sites — websites that are packaged up and served as static HTML files and assets from a CDN. These static sites might exist as a collection of HTML files in a code repository, or they may be generated using a static site generator or build script, where a set of instructions as code turn template code into static HTML files and store them on a CDN.

    Jamstack was originally named JAMstack with uppercase JAM to describe the combination of technologies mentioned above. It has since moved to Jamstack to reflect its evolution to an architectural approach, instead of describing the technologies that might be used. You don’t necessarily have to use APIs — or even JavaScript — to build a Jamstack site!

    That’s the terminology covered, but what was different about Jamstack when it emerged in 2015?

    Jamstack made websites faster

    Traditionally, requests for web pages were processed by a managed server (more on this later), which did the job of building HTML documents in real-time before sending it back to the user’s browser. If the web page was big and complex, and if the page request was far away from the origin server, this meant that websites could be slow, resulting in a bad experience for the end-user. As more and more people started to use the web for everything, a fast experience was key to making websites usable, accessible — especially on slower internet connections, making sales, distributing information, and growing your business.

    Jamstack solved this problem in two ways. Firstly, Jamstack sites centre on pre-generating and storing website pages in advance — meaning a server doesn’t need to build anything at the time of a page request. Secondly, these pre-generated static assets are served from a Content Delivery Network (CDN) — a network of servers distributed around the world, working together to serve cached content to users from the closest server location possible. This guarantees a much faster experience than the traditional request, build, and serve model from a fixed server location. (It’s worth mentioning here that the traditional architecture described above is still alive and kicking, and Jamstack provides an alternative way of working, should it fit the needs of the project or product.)

    Jamstack made developers faster

    Before Jamstack, websites were built using entire full stack frameworks that comprised the back end server code, front end templates, databases — everything. New product features could take weeks to build, test, and release to production, software upgrades could be risky and difficult to roll back, and development environments were difficult to provision. Jamstack shifted to a decoupled architecture. This means back end API services, front end templates, databases, and all other services are separated and streamlined, allowing developers of different disciplines to build, test and release in parallel with each other, and go faster.

    Jamstack sites are front end (client) code that may or may not speak to a separate back end server and/or APIs. During the build process, a Jamstack site can call out to any number of external API services to fetch data to pre-generate static pages. Once a Jamstack site is built, it is cached as an immutable deploy — a set of static files that cannot be changed. This means that a Jamstack site in production can be rolled back and forward quickly and easily between deploys should the need arise. What’s more, a breaking back end API change would have no effect on a static Jamstack site until the next time the site is rebuilt, making Jamstack sites more stable and less prone to bugs once they’re deployed to production. Speaking from experience, this decoupled approach also empowers front end developers to have full ownership of their code, processes and practices, without being at the mercy of a massive monolithic full stack scary codebase.

    Jamstack made scaling less complicated

    During the time of the massive monolith and the request-build-serve model, development and IT operations teams were responsible for managing their own servers (hence, “managed server” above). This came with a number of challenges, including managing security and software upgrades, DNS (!), and scaling the availability of servers up or down when website traffic increased or decreased. Jamstack hosting providers take care of all of this for you, meaning you can focus on writing the code that builds your product, instead of stressing about the infrastructure.

    This has also greatly lowered the bar for entry to building a website and putting it live for the world to enjoy. With Jamstack, even a beginner developer can put a website live in a matter of minutes. Write a static HTML file, put it on a CDN, automatically get a URL and SSL certificate, and share it with your friends.

    Adjacent Jamstack technologies

    Remember — Jamstack is an architectural way of working. Jamstack does not dictate what technologies, frameworks or cloud hosting providers you choose. All Jamstack requires is that you centre your project on the concept of serving static assets from a CDN. You can choose to use serverless functions, edge functions, content management systems, other API services and more to enhance and enrich your Jamstack site — but at the core of Jamstack is a static-first approach without a managed server.

    How to get started with your first Jamstack site

    Jamstack projects have the following characteristics:

    • Ready-to-serve assets
    • Any interactivity provided without a managed server
    • Any interactivity provided by some combination of JavaScript and decoupled services via APIs

    If you’re brand new to Jamstack, I personally don’t recommend starting out with a static site generator right away. Instead, I advise starting as small as possible. A Jamstack site can be as simple as:

    • serving a single HTML file (a ready-to-serve asset)
    • from a cloud hosting provider such as Netlify (no managed server)
    • that calls out to an external API on the client to get something done (if it needs to)

    And the great thing about Jamstack is when you’re ready to add new features and functionality to your site, you don’t need to start again from scratch. Add extra pages when you need to, maybe add in a static site generator or build tool to generate dynamic pages at build time. Add extra functionality when you need to; maybe you’ll use adjacent technologies like serverless functions — if it fits.

    There’s no magic formula for Jamstack, but at its core, a Jamstack site is a set of pre-generated static assets served from a CDN. So, choose your project, choose your cloud hosting provider, start small, relish in the glow of serving static files from a CDN, and enjoy the ride.

Items.Author: justacodergirl

Items.Title: 制作出色 CLI 应用程序的 14 个重要技巧 | 14 great tips to make amazing CLI applications
Items.Description:
我发现命令行工具是一种令人难以置信的方式来增加它们产生的代码的影响。

您是否编写了一个不错的库来包装 API 并将其连接到您的数据库模型？ 然后提供一个 CLI 工具，允许任何人查询 API、与 DB 模型交互、运行您提供的操作、发现所有可用功能并阅读 API 文档。 您现在可以轻松地编写脚本，在开发时执行您的代码的不同功能，创建预提交脚本，创建 CICD 脚本。

以下是我多年来收集的编写 CLI 工具的技巧。

使用命令行解析框架

虽然手动解析argv很有趣，但我直接使用全功能的命令行解析框架，例如 go 中的cobra 、 clap in rust、
在 python 中单击，在 PHP 中单击php-cli 。

这些不仅完善，它们通常允许您使用子命令，为多个 shell 生成自动完成脚本，处理 CLI 选项的漂亮打印并链接到配置框架（见下文）。

将工具作为单个文件分发，没有依赖关系

为确保您的 CLI 工具得到广泛采用，它必须易于安装。 没有人想安装 80 个节点模块或上帝保佑，设置一个 python virtualenv，只是为了使用你的工具。

  • 您还可以尝试在 Common Lisp 中转储世界图像。
  • 另一种选择是创建一个可以通过单个 docker 调用运行的 docker 映像，尽管这通常会使网络和文件 IO 更加复杂。
  • 一个折衷方案是通过你的语言包管理器（pip、npm、你有什么）使它成为一个易于安装的包

使文档成为工具本身的一部分

与其将文档作为单独的 wiki 分发，不如将其作为您工具的一部分。 不要吐出神秘的自动生成的参数列表，而是利用您的 CLI 框架和 TUI 库来提供漂亮的文档。

应该可以使用 CLI 工具来：

  • 查找有关动词和参数的所有信息（请参阅有关示例的下一点）
  • 获取有关工具本身及其操作原理的信息
  • 发现该工具提供的功能（请参阅有关构建语法的部分）

虽然我从未这样做过，但有些工具甚至提供了完整的教程作为其功能集的一部分！

考虑使用漂亮的库直接在命令行上呈现 markdown，例如glamor (golang) 或rich (python)。

将示例添加到您的文档中

帮助人们开始使用您的工具的最简单方法是提供大量示例。 这通常可以更直观地描述参数以及它们如何相互作用。 用它作为展示某些模式的机会，并提供一些关于为什么某个动词存在的背景信息。 以此为契机，展示使用 CLI 的复杂和高级方法，尤其是在您提供过滤和模板功能的情况下（见下文）。

让它漂亮

谁不喜欢漂亮多彩的 CLI 工具。 那里有很多终端 TUI 和样式管理库。 但是，不要忘记使您的工具与无色终端和 IO 管道兼容。

我在所有的charmbracelet库上玩得很开心，比如用于构建TUI 的bubbletea和用于造型的lipbgloss 。 使其具有交互性

对于某些应用程序（不是全部），我发现实现 CLI 工具的交互式版本很有用。 通常，我在控制硬件时会这样做，其中保持上下文很重要，并且在长时间运行的应用程序中更容易。

这可以像使用rlwrap包装一个简单的 stdin/stdout 循环一样简单，一直到使用功能齐全的 TUI 库，如bubbletea (golang)、 textual (python) 或imtui (c++)。

使用 asciinema 录制短教程

命令行应用程序的一大特点是记录终端会话非常轻量级，可以轻松转换为 gif 或呈现为网页。

像asciinema这样的工具非常适合展示现实生活中的工作流程。

精心制作 CLI 语法

这是我在构建 CLI 工具时一直发现的最困难的事情：设计一个好的语法。 在最好的情况下，动词和子命令是“直观的”：如果用户想要做某事，他们应该很容易猜出使用哪个命令。

这意味着如果您有一个get命令，则有一个set命令，或者对于每个可以操作的资源（例如files 、 hosts 、 drives ）都有一个适当的子命令。

一个令人困惑的语法是使用get --update来设置一个变量，或者使用动词ls-files来列出文件，使用hosts list子命令和动词来列出主机。

我发现我经常需要重写 CLI 动词结构几次，然后才能理解什么对我想要解决的问题有效。 不要试图在第一次就做好，事实上要避免第一次过于“框架化”或过于聪明。 在构建工具时随意添加您认为有用的动词和标志，然后在不介意从头开始重新启动动词和标志的地方进行第二次传递。 您可能会构建可重用的内部辅助函数。

添加结构化输入和输出

为了更轻松地传输和转换工具生成的数据，请考虑将结构化数据与人类可读数据一起输出。

我有生成的通用包装器：

  • 格式良好（和彩色）的表格化、人类可读的输出
  • 不同的预配置人类可读输出选项（例如--wide 、 --pretty 、 --oneline ）
  • 结构化 JSON 输出（这通常是最简单的，因为 CLI 操作的对象通常可以开箱即用地序列化）
  • 适用时，将数据作为 SQLite 数据库转储非常有用

类似地，不仅可以从 CLI，还可以从文件中轻松读取结构化输入。 这使得重新运行类似命令或在团队成员之间共享大批量数据变得更加容易。

有关这些概念的良好示例，请查看kubectl ，用于管理 kubernetes 资源。

添加过滤和聚合选项

结合前面添加结构化 IO 的概念，考虑添加过滤选项。 例如，您可以让用户选择仅将 3 个属性输出为表格数据，或按某个其他属性聚合计数。

作为实现您自己的过滤和聚合的替代方法，这并不总是那么容易，请考虑在您的 CLI 文档中使用jq 、 yq或xsv等工具添加过滤和处理数据的示例。

不要忘记提供大量示例（对现实世界越有用和适用越好），因为作为用户提出这些示例通常很耗时，尤其是当您只需要工具来完成某事时。 把你给出的每一个有用的例子想象成某人将来会体验到的一点快乐。

添加模板选项

如果您想进一步超越结构化输出，请考虑为用户提供提供输出模板的选项。 我总是发现为 CLI 编写模板非常耗时，但一旦我弄明白了它就非常有用。 所以不要吝啬例子！

轻松扩展您的 CLI

轻松添加新动词/扩展 CLI 工具。 这样做的一种常见模式是在使用未知动词XXX时查找名为yourtool-XXX的二进制文件是否存在，并将控制权传递给该二进制文件。 这就是gh （GitHub 客户端）和git的操作方式。

即使是简单的 shell 脚本动词也为用户提供了很好的扩展能力。 为了更大的影响，提供一个像gh这样的简单包装框架，它允许用 go 编写的扩展轻松调用主工具本身。

轻松配置 CLI

您可能希望在 CLI 中提供大量默认值和配置选项（凭据、URL 端点、输出设置）。

通过提供以下内容可以轻松配置这些：

  • 环境变量。 这具有使您的工具易于集成到 CICD 中的额外好处，并通过避免在命令行中列出凭据来提供安全性。 它还允许使用direnv 之类的工具轻松自定义每个目录或项目。
  • 配置文件。 一些命令行选项库提供了在命令行解析器旁边使用配置文件的选项（例如 golang 中的viper ）。 否则，使用 JSON、YAML 或 TOML 解析器读取默认值和覆盖没有什么比这更容易的了。
  • 提供动词来查询和更新您的配置文件。 许多现代 CLI 工具提供动词来与它们自己的配置进行交互。 很好的例子是git config ，它提供了一个丰富的动词来查询、删除、更新、设置 git 配置。 转储和查询当前信息（连同版本、平台、加载的环境和配置文件）可以轻松调试问题。
  • 提供全局和本地配置文件。 除了提供全局配置文件外，还允许用户提供本地配置文件。 使链接配置文件变得容易，这样您就可以拥有一个全局配置，然后是一个项目范围的配置（检入 git），然后是一个用户特定的覆盖配置（未检入 git）。

提供自动完成集成

对于用户来说，没有什么比按下 Tab 键并获得一个不错的自动完成更好的了。 如今，Shell 为自动完成提供了非常好的 UI，并提供了一个丰富的框架来利用这些 UI。

除了通过 CLI 参数解析框架使用内置的自动完成生成（见上文），考虑提供动态自动完成选项。 我过去在提供硬件控制工具时使用它来动态查询连接的设备。 自动完成将按 ID 显示已连接小部件的列表。

结论

它们通常很容易编写，对作者自己非常有用，可以作为在开发时练习代码的方法，也是“自我记录”和扩展应用程序库功能的好方法。

稍加思考和设计，CLI 就可以改变游戏规则。

那你呢？ 你会写 CLI 工具吗？ 如果是这样，您使用了哪些技巧？ 您喜欢 TUI 和交互性吗？



原文:

I find command-line tools an incredibly way to multiply the impact of the code they produce.

Did you write a nice library to wrap an API and connect it to your DB model? Then provide a CLI tool that allows anybody to query the API, interact with the DB model, run the operations that you provided, discover all the available functionality and read the API documentation. You can now easily write scripts that exercise different functionality of your code while developing, create pre-commit scripts, create CICD scripts.

Here are a collection of tips I gathered over the years writing CLI tools.

Use a command-line parsing framework

While it is fun to parse argv by hand, I go straight for full-featured command-line parsing frameworks like cobra in go, clap in rust,
click in python, php-cli in PHP.

Not only are these well established, they often allow you to use subcommands, generate autocompletion script for multiple shells, handle pretty-printing of CLI options and link up to configuration frameworks (see below).

Distribute the tool as a single file with no dependencies

To ensure widespread adoption of your CLI tool, it must be easily installable. No one wants to install 80 node modules or god forbid, setup a python virtualenv, just to use your tool.

  • Golang and Rust are perfect languages for this, as they allow you to easily compile static binaries for multiple platforms. You can also experiment with dumping world images in Common Lisp.
  • Another option is to create a docker image that can be run with a single docker call, although this often makes networking and file IO more complicated.
  • A compromise is to make it an easily installable package through your languages package manager (pip, npm, what have you)

Make documentation part of the tool itself

Instead of distributing the documentation as a separate wiki, make it part of your tool. Don't spit out cryptic auto-generated argument lists, but leverage your CLI framework and TUI library to provide beautiful documentation.

It should be possible to use the CLI tool to:

  • lookup all the information about verbs and arguments (see the next point about examples)
  • get information about the tool itself and its principles of operation
  • discover the functionality provided by the tool (see the section about a building a grammar)

While I have never done so, some tools even provide full tutorials as part of their feature set!

As complex the git command line can be, it provides great documentation entries for each verb.

Consider using a pretty library to render markdown straight on the command line, for example glamour (golang) or rich (python).

Add examples to your documentation

The easiest way to help people get started with your tool is to provide numerous examples. This often gives a more intuitive description of arguments and how they interact. Use it as an opportunity to show certain patterns and give some background information about why a certain verb exists. Use it as an opportunity to also show complex and advanced ways of using your CLI, especially if you provide filtering and templating functionality (see below).

git, aws, kubectl all provide examples that I have found extremely useful.

Make it pretty

Who doesn't like a pretty colorful CLI tool. There are a lot of terminal TUI and style management libraries out there. Don't forget to make your tool compatible to colorless terminals and IO pipes however.

I had a great time with all the charmbracelet libraries, like bubbletea for building TUIs and lipbgloss for styling. rich is a similar, awesome python library.

Make it interactive

For some applications (not all), I found implementing an interactive version of the CLI tool useful. Often, I have done so when controlling hardware, where keeping context is important, and much easier in a long running application.

This can be as easy as wrapping a simple stdin/stdout loop with rlwrap, all the way to using full featured TUI libraries like bubbletea (golang), textual (python) or imtui (c++).

Use asciinema to record short tutorials

A great feature of command line applications is that recording a terminal session is very lightweight, and can easily be transformed into a gif or rendered into a webpage.

Tools like asciinema are great to show real-life workflows.

Carefully craft a CLI grammar

This is what I consistently find the most difficult when building a CLI tool: designing a nice grammar. In the best of worlds, verbs and subcommands are "intuitive": if a user wants to do something, it should be easy for them to guess which command to use.

This means having a set command if you have a get command, or a proper subcommand for each resource that can be manipulated (for example files, hosts, drives ).

A confusing grammar would be using get --update to set a variable, or having a verb ls-files for listing files, and a hosts list subcommand and verb for listing hosts.

I find that I often have to rewrite the CLI verb structure a couple of times before I understand what works well for the problem I want to solve. Don't try to get it right the first time, in fact avoiding being too "frameworky" or clever the first time around. Liberally add the verbs and flags you find useful as you are building out your tool, and then do a second pass where you don't mind restarting the verbs and flags from scratch. Chances are that you build internal helper functions that can be reused.

Add structured input and output

CLIs are not just for humans, they are also extremely useful for machines. To make it easier to pipe and convert the data your tools generate, think about outputting structured data alongside human-readable data.

I have generic wrappers that generate:

  • tabulated, human-readable output that is nicely formatted (and colored)
  • different pre-configured human readable output options (for example --wide, --pretty, --oneline)
  • CSV and TSV formats for easy unix tool / spreadsheet consumption
  • structured JSON output (this is usually the easiest, as the objects manipulated by the CLI are often serializable out of the box)
  • when applicable, dumping data out as a SQLite database is extremely useful

Similarly, make it easy to read structured input, not just from the CLI, but also from files. This makes it much easier to rerun similar commands or share big batches of data amongst team members.

For good example of these concepts, look at kubectl , used to managed kubernetes resources.

Add filtering and aggregation options

In conjunction with the previous concept of adding structured IO, consider adding filtering options. For example, you could give a user the option to only output 3 attributes as tabular data, or to aggregate counts by a certain other attribute.

As an alternative to implementing your own filtering and aggregating, which is not always easy, consider adding examples where you filter and process data by using tools like jq, yq or xsv in your CLI documentation.

Don't forget to give copious examples (the more useful and applicable to the real-world, the better), as these are often time-consuming to come up as a user, especially when you just need the tool to get something done. Think of each useful example you give as a little nugget of pleasure that someone will experience in the future.

Add templating options

If you want to go even a step further beyond structured output, consider giving the user the option to provide output templates. Tools like kubectl or aws allow you to pass your own go-template inspired templates, making it possible to create exactly the output needed.

kubectl get no -o go-template='{{range .items}}{{if .spec.unschedulable}}{{.metadata.name}} {{.spec.externalID}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}{{end}}'

I always found writing templates for CLIs quite time-consuming, but extremely useful once I figured it out. So don't skimp on examples!

Make it easy to extend your CLI

Make it easy to add new verbs / extend the CLI tool. A common pattern to do so is to lookup if a binary called yourtool-XXX exists when an unknown verb XXX is used, and pass control to that binary. This is how gh (GitHub client) and git operate.

Even simple shell script verbs provide great extension capability for users. For even bigger impact, provide a simple wrapper framework like gh does, that allows extensions written in go to easily call the main tool itself.

Make it easy to configure your CLI

Chances are that you will want to provide a lot of default values and configuration options in your CLI (credentials, URL endpoints, output settings).

Make it easy to configure these by providing:

  • environment variables. This has the added benefit of making your tool easy to integrate in CICD, and provide security by avoiding listing credentials in your command line. It also allows for easy customization per directory or project, using a tool like direnv.
  • configuration files. A few command line option libraries provide the option to use a configuration file alongside the command line parser (for example viper in golang). Otherwise, nothing is easier to use a JSON, YAML, or TOML parser to read in defaults and overrides.
  • provide verbs to query and update your config file. A lot of modern CLI tools provide verbs to interact with their own configuration. Good examples are git config, which provides a rich verb to query, remove, update, set the git configuration. Dumping and querying the current information (alongside version, platform, loaded environments and config files) makes it easy to debug issues.
  • provide global and local configuration files. Besides providing a global configuration file, allow users to provide local configuration files. Make it easy to chain configuration files, so that you can have a global config, followed by a project-wide config (checked into git), followed by a user specific override config (not checked into git).

Provide autocompletion integration

Nothing is nicer for the user than just pressing tab and getting a nice autocompletion. Shells these days provide really nice UIs for autocompletion, and a rich framework to leverage these.

Besides using the builtin autocompletion generation by CLI argument parsing frameworks (see above), think about providing dynamic autocompletion options. I have used this in the past to dynamically query connected devices when providing a hardware control tool. Autocompletion would show a list of connected widgets by ID.

Conclusion

CLIs and TUIs are incredibly effective tool for the developer. They are usually easy to write, extremely useful for the author themselves as ways to exercise their code while developing, and a great way to "self-document" and extend the functionality of the application our library.

With a bit of thought and styling, CLIs can be turned into game changers.

What about you? Do you write CLI tools? If so, what tricks have you been using? Are you into TUIs and interactivity?

Items.Title: 使用 GitHub 实施 CI/CD - 部署 Azure Functions | Implement CI/CD with GitHub - Deploy Azure Functions
Items.Description:
在今天的教程中，我们将通过使用 GitHub Actions 来自动化 Azure Function 部署来了解使用 GitHub 实现 CI/CD。

将我们的 Azure Functions 引入和维护到 Git 存储库还带来了版本控制、源代码管理以及通过 CI/CD 将我们的功能自动构建和部署到 Azure 中的所有好处。

先决条件

要开始，您需要做一些事情，首先：

  • 您还需要在您将使用的机器上安装一些本地先决条件。 就我而言，我将准备我的机器来开发C#函数，但您可以在这里查看其他一些代码堆栈： 运行本地要求

    • 适用于 Visual Studio 代码的 Azure 函数
    • 用于 Visual Studio 代码的 C#

    创建 Azure 函数应用

    让我们首先在我们的 Azure 订阅中创建一个资源组和一个 Windows dotnet 函数应用程序。 对于这一步，我使用 Azure CLI 编写了一个 PowerShell 脚本。 您还可以在我的GitHub 存储库中找到这个脚本。

     #Log into Azure
az login # Setup Variables.
$randomInt = Get-Random -Maximum 9999
$subscriptionId = $ ( az account show --query id -o tsv )
$resourceGroupName = "GitHub-Managed-Function-Demo"
$storageName = "demofuncsa $randomInt "
$functionAppName = "demofunc $randomInt "
$region = "uksouth" # Create a resource resourceGroupName
az group create --name " $resourceGroupName " --location " $region " # Create an azure storage account for function app
az storage account create ` --name " $storageName " ` --location " $region " ` --resource-group " $resourceGroupName " ` --sku "Standard_LRS" ` --kind "StorageV2" ` --https-only true ` --min-tls-version "TLS1_2" # Create a Function App
az functionapp create ` --name " $functionAppName " ` --storage-account " $storageName " ` --consumption-plan-location " $region " ` --resource-group " $resourceGroupName " ` --os-type "Windows" ` --runtime "dotnet" ` --runtime-version "6" ` --functions-version "4" ` --assign-identity

    上述脚本创建了一个资源组，其中包含函数应用、函数应用存储和见解以及消费应用服务计划。

    注意：我们在这个阶段只创建了我们的函数应用程序，我们还没有任何函数。 我们稍后将在本教程中使用 GitHub 创建我们的第一个函数。

    创建 GitHub 存储库

    接下来，转到您的 GitHub 帐户并创建一个新存储库。 稍后我们将使用此存储库链接我们的函数 app/s 源代码。

    我已经调用了我的存储库Demo-Azure-Functions

    准备当地要求

    如本文开头所述，我们现在将在我们将要工作和开发功能代码的机器上安装并运行一些先决条件。

    安装以下工具：

    克隆 GitHub 函数库

    现在安装了我们所有的工具，我们现在可以将我们的 GitHub 函数存储库克隆到我们的本地机器：

    1. 复制克隆 URL

    2. 打开 VSCode 并导航到命令面板

    3. 在命令面板中输入克隆并点击Git:clone

    4. 粘贴复制的克隆 URL ，然后选择要将存储库克隆到的文件夹。 将 Azure Function App 与 GitHub 存储库链接

      接下来，我们将在本地克隆的存储库中创建一个空文件夹。 该文件夹将代表我们的Function App ：

      注意：我在我的存储库中调用了我的文件夹，其名称与我之前创建的 Azure Function App 的名称相同； 演示函数6144 。

      现在我们将使用我们之前安装的 Visual Studio Code 的 Azure Functions 扩展在文件夹内创建我们的第一个函数。

      在 VSCode 中，您将看到安装在屏幕左侧的扩展程序。 单击扩展并选择Create New Project ：

      这将再次打开命令面板，浏览并选择我们创建的代表函数应用程序的空文件夹：

      命令面板现在将为您提供一些选项，请选择以下选项：

      1. 选择语言： C#

      2. 选择 .NET 运行时： .NET 6

      3. 为项目的第一个函数选择一个模板： HTTP 触发器

      4. 为函数命名： MyFirstDotnetFunction

      5. 提供命名空间： My.Function

      6. 选择适当的访问权限：匿名/功能/管理员

      完成上述过程后，请注意，现在我们的文件夹demofunc6144中有一个C#函数模板，我们可以立即开始工作。 因为这也在我们的本地git 存储库中，所以我们可以确保我们的代码始终通过源代码控制进行管理。

      保存并提交更改，然后将新函数推送到远程GitHub 存储库：

      部署函数应用

      现在我们有了一个完全集成的工作区，我们可以使用它来创建和开发Functions 。 但是我们还没有设置任何 CI/CD。

      这将我们带到最后一步，使用GitHub Actions使用 CI/CD 自动部署我们的函数

      1. 导航回我们之前创建的托管在 Azure 中的Function App ，然后转到部署中心：

      2. 选择 Source GitHub并设置我们创建的Org 、 Repo和Branch ，然后点击Save 。 注意：您还可以通过上述步骤管理发布配置文件。

        当您保存上述配置时，您会注意到GitHub 存储库上有一个自动设置的新自动化工作流以及一个新的存储库密码。

        工作流将位于 Azure 自动创建的名为.github/workflows的特殊文件夹中：

        在我的情况下，工作流程称为master_decomfunc6144.yml ：

        让我们仔细看看这个工作流程：

         name : Build and deploy dotnet core app to Azure Function App - demofunc6144 on : push : branches : - master paths : - ' demofunc6144/**' workflow_dispatch : env : AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH : ' demofunc6144' # set this to the path to your web app project, defaults to the repository root DOTNET_VERSION : ' 6.0.x' # set this to the dotnet version to use jobs : build-and-deploy : runs-on : windows-latest steps : - name : ' Checkout GitHub Action' uses : actions/checkout@v2 - name : Setup DotNet ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} Environment uses : actions/setup-dotnet@v1 with : dotnet-version : ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} - name : ' Resolve Project Dependencies Using Dotnet' shell : pwsh run : | pushd './${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}' dotnet build --configuration Release --output ./output popd - name : ' Run Azure Functions Action' uses : Azure/functions-action@v1 id : fa with : app-name : ' demofunc6144' slot-name : ' Production' package : ' ${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}/output' publish-profile : ${{ secrets.AZUREAPPSERVICE_PUBLISHPROFILE_AD8BBBC377A040C480EB918BC04CE61C }}

        注意：我添加了工作流触发器，仅在我的主分支上触发，并且在文件夹/repo 路径demofunc6144下进行的任何更改。 另请注意，我已将函数应用程序包路径的环境变量更改为demofunc6144

         #Environment variables env : AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH : ' demofunc6144' # set this to the path to your web app project, defaults to the repository root DOTNET_VERSION : ' 6.0.x' # set this to the dotnet version to use

        让我们看一下这个自动化工作流在触发时会做什么：

         jobs : build-and-deploy : runs-on : windows-latest steps : - name : ' Checkout GitHub Action' uses : actions/checkout@v2 - name : Setup DotNet ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} Environment uses : actions/setup-dotnet@v1 with : dotnet-version : ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} - name : ' Resolve Project Dependencies Using Dotnet' shell : pwsh run : | pushd './${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}' dotnet build --configuration Release --output ./output popd - name : ' Run Azure Functions Action' uses : Azure/functions-action@v1 id : fa with : app-name : ' demofunc6144' slot-name : ' Production' package : ' ${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}/output' publish-profile : ${{ secrets.AZUREAPPSERVICE_PUBLISHPROFILE_AD8BBBC377A040C480EB918BC04CE61C }}

        上面的工作基本上有4个步骤：

        1. 代码已签出到 GitHub 运行器上。
        2. 我们在环境变量中指定的 .NET 版本将安装在 GitHub 运行器上。
        3. 使用函数应用的发布配置文件将函数部署到 Azure 函数应用demofunc6144 ：
         publish-profile : ${{ secrets.AZUREAPPSERVICE_PUBLISHPROFILE_AD8BBBC377A040C480EB918BC04CE61C }}

        请注意，发布配置文件实际上存储为 GitHub Action Secret，这也是 Azure 作为工作流 YAML 文件的一部分自动创建的：

        注意：这个 Actions Secret 基本上是 Function Apps Publish Profile File的内容，如果需要，可以手动下载和重新添加：

        让我们手动触发此工作流并将我们的函数部署到 Azure Function App 中。 在 GitHub 中导航到 Actions，选择工作流，然后选择Run Workflow ：

        在运行工作流之后，我们现在可以在 Azure 上的 Function App 中看到我们的 Function。

        结论

        就是这样，现在我们已经成功地将我们的函数应用程序开发生命周期使用源代码控制与 Git 和 GitHub 集成，并且能够使用 CI/CD 工作流和 GitHub Actions 自动部署我们的函数。

        我们可以简单地为任何新的函数应用创建额外的文件夹，以及链接到在 Azure 中为相关函数应用创建的部署函数的相应 YAML 工作流。

        我希望你喜欢这篇文章并学到了一些新东西。 您还可以在我发布的Github Action页面上找到这篇博文中使用的代码示例。 作者

        喜欢，分享，关注我：🐙 GitHub | 约翰内斯·凯特曼 - Mar 18 3月18日



原文:

In todays tutorial we will take a look at implementing CI/CD with GitHub by using GitHub Actions to automate Azure Function deployment.

Bringing and maintaining our Azure Functions into a Git repository also brings all the benefits of version control, source code management and automated build and deployment of our functions into Azure though CI/CD.

Pre-requisites

To get started you'll need a few things, firstly:

  • An Azure Subscription
  • A GitHub Account and Git repository

You will also need to install a few local pre-requirements on the machine you will be working on. In my case I will prepare my machine for developing C# Functions, but you can take a look at some of the other code stacks here: Run Local Requirements

  • Azure Functions Core Tools
  • VSCode
  • Azure Functions for Visual Studio Code
  • C# for Visual Studio Code

Create an Azure Function App

Lets start by creating a resource group and a windows dotnet function app in our Azure subscription. For this step I have written a PowerShell script using Azure CLI. You can also find ths script on my GitHub repository.

#Log into Azure
az login # Setup Variables.
$randomInt = Get-Random -Maximum 9999
$subscriptionId = $(az account show --query id -o tsv)
$resourceGroupName = "GitHub-Managed-Function-Demo"
$storageName = "demofuncsa$randomInt"
$functionAppName = "demofunc$randomInt"
$region = "uksouth" # Create a resource resourceGroupName
az group create --name "$resourceGroupName" --location "$region" # Create an azure storage account for function app
az storage account create `
 --name "$storageName" `
 --location "$region" `
 --resource-group "$resourceGroupName" `
 --sku "Standard_LRS" `
 --kind "StorageV2" `
 --https-only true `
 --min-tls-version "TLS1_2" # Create a Function App
az functionapp create `
 --name "$functionAppName" `
 --storage-account "$storageName" `
 --consumption-plan-location "$region" `
 --resource-group "$resourceGroupName" `
 --os-type "Windows" `
 --runtime "dotnet" `
 --runtime-version "6" `
 --functions-version "4" `
 --assign-identity

The above script created a resource group containing the function app, function app storage and insights as well as the consumption app service plan.

NOTE: We have only created our Function App at this stage, we do not have any Functions yet. We will create our first function later on in this tutorial with GitHub.

Create a GitHub repository

Next, head over to your GitHub account and create a new repository. We will use this repository to link our function app/s source code later.

I have called my repository Demo-Azure-Functions

Prepare Local Requirements

As mentioned at the beginning of this post we will now install and run a few pre-requirements on the machine we will be working and developing our function code on.

Install the following tools:

Clone GitHub Function repository

With all our tools now installed we can now clone our GitHub function repository to our local machine:

  1. Copy the clone URL

  2. Open VSCode and navigate to the Command Palette

  3. In the command palette type clone and click on Git:clone

  4. Paste in the copied clone URL and select a folder you want to clone the repository to. (Note: The repo will be cloned to a sub folder in the folder you selected, the name of this sub folder will match the repo name and will contain all your repo files.)

Link Azure Function App with GitHub Repository

Next we will create an empty folder inside of our locally cloned repository. This folder will represent our Function App:

NOTE: I have called my folder in my repo the same name as the name I have given to my Azure Function App we created earlier; demofunc6144.

Now we will create our first function inside of the folder using the Azure Functions extension for Visual Studio Code we installed earlier.

In VSCode you will see the extension installed on the left side of the screen. Click on the extension and select Create New Project:

This will then open the Command Palette again, browse to and select the empty folder we created representing our Function App:

The Command Palette will now present you with some options, select the following:

  1. Select a language: C#

  2. Select a .NET runtime: .NET 6

  3. Select a template for your project’s first function: HTTP trigger

  4. Give the function a name: MyFirstDotnetFunction

  5. Provide a namespace: My.Function

  6. Select appropriate access rights: Anonymous/Function/Admin

Once the above process has completed, notice that now we have a C# function template in our folder demofunc6144 we can straight away start working on. Because this is also in our local git repository we can ensure that our code is always managed through source control.

Save and commit the the changes, then push the new function to the remote GitHub repository:

Deploy Function App

Now we have a fully integrated workspace we can use to create and develop Functions. But we have not set up any CI/CD yet.

This brings us to the last step, automating the deployment of our Functions with CI/CD using GitHub Actions

  1. Navigate back to the Function App hosted in Azure that we created earlier and go to Deployment Center:

  2. Select Source GitHub and set the Org, Repo and Branch we created and hit Save. (NOTE: You will be asked to link your GitHub account if you are performing this step for the very first time):

NOTE: You can also manage the Publish Profile from the above step.

When you save the configuration above, you will notice that on the GitHub repository there is a new automation workflow that is automatically set up as well as a new repository secret.

The workflow will be in a special folder called .github/workflows that is automatically created by Azure:

In my case the workflow is called master_decomfunc6144.yml:

Let's take a closer look at this workflow:

name: Build and deploy dotnet core app to Azure Function App - demofunc6144 on: push: branches: - master paths: - 'demofunc6144/**' workflow_dispatch: env: AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH: 'demofunc6144' # set this to the path to your web app project, defaults to the repository root DOTNET_VERSION: '6.0.x' # set this to the dotnet version to use jobs: build-and-deploy: runs-on: windows-latest steps: - name: 'Checkout GitHub Action' uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Setup DotNet ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} Environment uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v1 with: dotnet-version: ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} - name: 'Resolve Project Dependencies Using Dotnet' shell: pwsh run: | pushd './${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}' dotnet build --configuration Release --output ./output popd - name: 'Run Azure Functions Action' uses: Azure/functions-action@v1 id: fa with: app-name: 'demofunc6144' slot-name: 'Production' package: '${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}/output' publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZUREAPPSERVICE_PUBLISHPROFILE_AD8BBBC377A040C480EB918BC04CE61C }}

NOTE: I have added the workflow trigger to only trigger on my master branch and for any changes made under the folder/repo path demofunc6144. The workflow_dispatch: trigger allows us to additionally trigger and run the automation workflow manually.

#Trigger
on: push: branches: - master paths: - 'demofunc6144/**' workflow_dispatch:

Also note that I have changed the environment variables for the function app package path to demofunc6144

#Environment variables
env: AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH: 'demofunc6144' # set this to the path to your web app project, defaults to the repository root DOTNET_VERSION: '6.0.x' # set this to the dotnet version to use

Let's take a look at what this automation workflow will do when it is triggered:

jobs: build-and-deploy: runs-on: windows-latest steps: - name: 'Checkout GitHub Action' uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Setup DotNet ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} Environment uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v1 with: dotnet-version: ${{ env.DOTNET_VERSION }} - name: 'Resolve Project Dependencies Using Dotnet' shell: pwsh run: | pushd './${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}' dotnet build --configuration Release --output ./output popd - name: 'Run Azure Functions Action' uses: Azure/functions-action@v1 id: fa with: app-name: 'demofunc6144' slot-name: 'Production' package: '${{ env.AZURE_FUNCTIONAPP_PACKAGE_PATH }}/output' publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZUREAPPSERVICE_PUBLISHPROFILE_AD8BBBC377A040C480EB918BC04CE61C }}

The above job basically has 4 steps:

  1. The code is checked out onto the GitHub runner.
  2. The version of .NET we specified in the environment variables will be installed on the GitHub runner.
  3. The Azure Function is built and packaged
  4. The Function is deployed to the Azure Function App, demofunc6144 using the publish-profile of the Function App: 
publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZUREAPPSERVICE_PUBLISHPROFILE_AD8BBBC377A040C480EB918BC04CE61C }}

Note that the Publish Profile is actually stored as a GitHub Action Secret, this was also automatically created by Azure as part of the workflow YAML file:

NOTE: This Actions Secret is basically the contents of the Function Apps Publish Profile File which can be downloaded and re-added if ever needed manually:

Let's trigger this workflow manually and deploy our function into the Azure Function App. In GitHub navigate to Actions, select the workflow and then Run Workflow:

After the workflow as ran, we can now see our Function in the Function App on Azure.

Conclusion

That's all there is to it, now we have successfully integrated our function app development lifecycle using source control with Git and GitHub and have the ability to automatically deploy our Functions using CI/CD workflows with GitHub Actions.

We can simply create additional folders for any new Function Apps along with a corresponding YAML workflow linked to deploy functions created for the relevant Function Apps in Azure.

I hope you have enjoyed this post and have learned something new. You can also find the code samples used in this blog post on my published Github Action page. ❤️

Author

Like, share, follow me on: 🐙 GitHub | 🐧 Twitter | 👾 LinkedIn

Johannes Kettmann - Mar 18

Items.Title: 调用 Lambda 和 StepFunction 的 EventBridge 规则 | EventBridge Rules to Invoke Lambda and StepFunction
Items.Description:
一个服务可以发布一个事件（即，一条消息）并且多个其他服务可以订阅该事件。

订阅机制是通过单独的规则来完成的，这些规则定义要在事件总线上捕获的事件的模式，并定义要在该事件上调用的多个目标，例如 Lambda 函数或 StepFunction 状态机。

动机

这篇文章旨在为如何使用规则调用这两个服务以及如何处理典型的授权挑战提供蓝图。 所需资源以 JSON 格式定义为 CloudFormation 模板，但可以轻松转换为 YAML。

我创建了一个测试 Lambda 函数和一个测试 StepFunction 状态机，由它们各自的 EventBridge 规则调用。 我将 CloudFormation 中的这些资源称为TestLambdaFunction和TestStateMachine ，但这些只是它们的逻辑 ID，在部署时将替换为 ARN。

调用 StepFunction 状态机

此 CloudFormation 模板定义了一个 ID InvokeStateMachineEventRule且名称为start-state-machine的事件规则，该规则在default事件总线上侦听detail-type设置为start-state-machine的事件。

目标是触发规则时调用的资源。 内部函数Fn::GetAtt返回指定为目标资源的TestStateMachine的 ARN。
此外，每个目标都必须将 IAM 角色定义为RoleArn ，用于调用资源。

 { "InvokeStateMachineEventRule" : { "Type" : "AWS::Events::Rule" , "Properties" : { "EventBusName" : "default" , "EventPattern" : { "detail-type" : [ "start-state-machine" ], }, "Name" : "start-state-machine" , "State" : "ENABLED" , "Targets" : [ { "Arn" : { "Fn: :GetAtt" : [ "TestStateMachine" , "Arn" ] }, "Id" : "TestStateMachine" , "RoleArn" : { "Fn: :GetAtt" : [ "InvokeStateMachineIamRole" , "Arn" ] }, }, ], }, },
}

由于 EventBridge 规则使用该角色来调用另一个服务资源（即 StepFunction 状态机），因此提供了一个额外的AssumeRolePolicyDocument以允许从 EventBridge（即 principal events.amazonaws.com ）中获取该角色。

 { "InvokeStateMachineIamRole" : { "Type" : "AWS::IAM::Role" , "Properties" : { "Policies" : [ { "PolicyName" : "InvokeStateMachineRolePolicy" , "PolicyDocument" : { "Version" : "2012-10-17" , "Statement" : [ { "Effect" : "Allow" , "Action" : [ "states:StartExecution" ], "Resource" : [ { "Fn: :GetAtt" : [ "TestStateMachine" , "Arn" ] } ], }, ], }, }, ], }, }, "AssumeRolePolicyDocument" : { "Version" : "2012-10-17" , "Statement" : [ { "Effect" : "Allow" , "Principal" : { "Service" : [ "events.amazonaws.com" ], }, "Action" : "sts:AssumeRole" , }, ], },
}

调用 Lambda 函数

此事件规则InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule与上一个类似，不同之处在于它被命名为start-lambda-function并侦听detail-type设置为start-lambda-function的事件。 此外，目标资源是TestLambdaFunction并且有意省略了RoleArn 。

 { "InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule" : { "Type" : "AWS::Events::Rule" , "Properties" : { "EventBusName" : "default" , "EventPattern" : { "detail-type" : [ "start-lambda-function" ], }, "Name" : "start-lambda-function" , "State" : "ENABLED" , "Targets" : [ { "Arn" : { "Fn: :GetAtt" : [ "TestLambdaFunction" , "Arn" ] }, "Id" : "TestLambdaFunction" , }, ], }, },
}

如果我们为TestLambdaFunction目标提供RoleArn ，它会在部署时抛出错误：

目标 arn:aws:lambda:eu-central-1:xxxxxxxxxxxx:function:eventbridge-rule-example-dev-test 不支持 RoleArn。

与其他服务不同，Lambda 使用基于资源的权限来允许其他 AWS 服务代表您调用此函数。 因此，我们需要为TestLambdaFunction资源（即函数名称）添加一个允许Lambda:InvokeFunction操作的InvokeLambdaFunctionPermission定义。 因此，我们需要指定主体events.amazonaws.com和实际调用该函数的InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule的 ARN。

 { "InvokeLambdaFunctionPermission" : { "Type" : "AWS::Lambda::Permission" , "Properties" : { "FunctionName" : { "Fn: :GetAtt" : [ "TestLambdaFunction" , "Arn" ], }, "Action" : "lambda:InvokeFunction" , "Principal" : "events.amazonaws.com" , "SourceArn" : { "Fn: :GetAtt" : [ "InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule" , "Arn" ] }, }, },
}

我使用无服务器框架部署了所有资源，但只是为了快速创建 Lambda 函数和 StepFunction 状态机。 所有重要资源都定义为 AWS CloudFormation 模板，并且在以其他方式部署时有效，例如 AWS SAM、AWS CLI 等。

随意查看完整模板并将其部署到您自己的 AWS 账户： GitHub 存储库



原文:

AWS EventBridge is an event bus system for loosely coupling different services and resources in a publish/subscribe pattern. A service can publish an event (i.e., a message) and multiple other services can subscribe to that event.

The subscription mechanism is done through individual rules that define the pattern of an event to be catched on the event bus and define multiple targets to be called upon that event, e.g. a Lambda function or a StepFunction state machine.

Motivation

This post is intended to serve as a blueprint for how to invoke these two services with rules and how to deal with typical authorization challenges along the way. The required resources are defined as a CloudFormation template in JSON, but can easily be converted to YAML.

I created a test Lambda function and a test StepFunction state machine to be called by their respective EventBridge rule. I refer to these resources in CloudFormation as TestLambdaFunction and TestStateMachine, but these are just their logical IDs and will be replaced with ARNs when deployed.

Invoke StepFunction State Machine

This CloudFormation template defines an event rule with ID InvokeStateMachineEventRule and name start-state-machine that listens on the default event bus for events whose detail-type is set to start-state-machine.

Targets are resources that are invoked when a rule is triggered. The intrinsic function Fn::GetAtt returns the ARN of TestStateMachine specified as the target resource.
Additionally, each target must define an IAM role as RoleArn which is used to invoke the resource.

{ "InvokeStateMachineEventRule": { "Type": "AWS::Events::Rule", "Properties": { "EventBusName": "default", "EventPattern": { "detail-type": ["start-state-machine"], }, "Name": "start-state-machine", "State": "ENABLED", "Targets": [ { "Arn": { "Fn: :GetAtt": ["TestStateMachine","Arn"] }, "Id": "TestStateMachine", "RoleArn": { "Fn: :GetAtt": ["InvokeStateMachineIamRole","Arn"] }, }, ], }, },
}

The IAM role is defined as InvokeStateMachineIamRole and contains an inline policy that allows the states:StartExecution action for the TestStateMachine resource. Since the role is used by an EventBridge rule to invoke another service resource (i.e. StepFunction state machine), an additional AssumeRolePolicyDocument is provided to allow the role to be taken from EventBridge (i. e. principal events.amazonaws.com).

{ "InvokeStateMachineIamRole": { "Type": "AWS::IAM::Role", "Properties": { "Policies": [ { "PolicyName": "InvokeStateMachineRolePolicy", "PolicyDocument": { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["states:StartExecution"], "Resource": [ { "Fn: :GetAtt": ["TestStateMachine", "Arn"] } ], }, ], }, }, ], }, }, "AssumeRolePolicyDocument": { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": ["events.amazonaws.com"], }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", }, ], },
}

Invoke Lambda Function

This event rule InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule is similar to the previous one, except that it is named start-lambda-function and listens for events whose detail-type is set to start-lambda-function. Also, the target resource is TestLambdaFunction and RoleArn was intentionally omitted.

{ "InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule": { "Type": "AWS::Events::Rule", "Properties": { "EventBusName": "default", "EventPattern": { "detail-type": ["start-lambda-function"], }, "Name": "start-lambda-function", "State": "ENABLED", "Targets": [ { "Arn": { "Fn: :GetAtt": ["TestLambdaFunction","Arn"] }, "Id": "TestLambdaFunction", }, ], }, },
}

If we provide a RoleArn for the TestLambdaFunction target, it will throw an error when deploying it:

UPDATE_FAILED: InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule (AWS::Events::Rule)
RoleArn is not supported for target arn:aws:lambda:eu-central-1:xxxxxxxxxxxx:function:eventbridge-rule-example-dev-test.

Unlike other services, Lambda uses resource-based permissions to allow other AWS services to invoke this function on your behalf. Therefore, we need to add an InvokeLambdaFunctionPermission definition that allows the Lambda:InvokeFunction action for the TestLambdaFunction resource (i.e. function name). Also this permission is used by an EventBridge rule to invoke this Lambda function. Therefore, we need to specify the principal events.amazonaws.com and the ARN of InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule that actually calls the function.

{ "InvokeLambdaFunctionPermission": { "Type": "AWS::Lambda::Permission", "Properties": { "FunctionName": { "Fn: :GetAtt": ["TestLambdaFunction", "Arn"], }, "Action": "lambda:InvokeFunction", "Principal": "events.amazonaws.com", "SourceArn": { "Fn: :GetAtt": ["InvokeLambdaFunctionEventRule", "Arn"] }, }, },
}

GitHub Repository

I deployed all resources using the Serverless Framework, but only to quickly create the Lambda function and StepFunction state machine. All the important resources are defined as AWS CloudFormation templates and are valid when deployed in other ways, e.g. AWS SAM, AWS CLI, etc.

Feel free to check out the full template and deploy it on your own AWS account: GitHub respository

Items.Title: Astro Turbolinks：快速的 AstroJS 导航 | Astro Turbolinks: fast AstroJS Navigation
Items.Description:
在这篇关于 Astro Turbolinks 的文章中，我们首先来看看 Turbolinks 是什么以及它们存在的原因。 然后，我们将了解如何将 Turbolinks 添加到您的 Astro JS 站点以进行快速导航。 你会看到它或多或少只是添加一个包和几行配置的情况。

当访问者单击您网站上的内部链接（指向同一网站上另一个页面的链接）时，浏览器会跟随该链接并请求新页面。 如果站点没有进行过优化，则在浏览器等待新页面时可能会出现一闪而过的白色内容。 这称为整页刷新。 使用 Turbolink，当访问者单击内部链接时，Turbolinks 会拦截该链接，而不是点击该链接。 然后它使用称为 XMLHttpRequest 的东西从服务器获取页面。 这是一种允许浏览器在不刷新整个页面的情况下获取新页面的方法。 当 Turbolinks 收到响应时，智能位就出现了。 它查找上一页和新页之间的差异。 接下来，它交换新页面上不同的内容，但保持相同的内容。 作为最后一步，Turbolinks 会更改浏览器的 url，使其与新呈现的页面匹配。

对于连接速度较慢的用户，Turbolinks 提供了用户体验增强。 通过避免整个页面刷新，更改看起来会更顺畅，允许用户在页面只有一小部分更改的情况下继续他们正在做的事情。

基本上要将它添加到您的 Astro JS 站点，您只需要安装一个包并更新配置。 您甚至可以自动化该设置！ 我们将看几个用例。 首先，使用自动设置将 Turbolinks 添加到新的 Astro 站点。 然后我们看看如何使用手动设置将它添加到现有的 Astro 项目中，尽管相当简单。

在此设置过程中，您可以从包括Generic和Blog以及Minimal在内的不同站点类型列表中进行选择。 以后完全定制您的项目并不太麻烦。 考虑到这一点，只需选择听起来最接近您正在做的事情的选项，然后从那里开始。 我们在此处禁用 Astro 遥测，如果您想保持启用，请跳过该行。

在下面一行中，我们添加了 Turbolinks。 这将自动提示您安装@astrojs/turbolinks包并将配置添加到astro.config.mjs 。 回答“是”以自动执行这些步骤。 在同一行中，我们正在为 Svelte 和 React 添加设置。 如果您不需要它们，请删除它们。 或者，通过在命令中添加lit 、 vue 、 solid或preact来介绍您可能需要的其他人。

就是这样！ 跳转到测试部分以检查它是否正常工作。

您也可以在现有项目上运行astro add ，但可能会选择手动进行设置。 第一步，安装@astrojs/turbolinks包：

 pnpm add -D @astrojs/turbolinks

然后你只需要更新astro.config.mjs ：

 import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config'; import turbolinks from '@astrojs/turbolinks'; import svelte from '@astrojs/svelte'; // https://astro.build/config export default defineConfig({ site: 'https://example.com', integrations: [svelte(), turbolinks()], });

这里我们导入我们刚刚安装的包，然后将它添加到任何现有的integrations中。

由于 Astro 已经如此之快，您可能无法察觉 Turbolinks 正在您的开发服务器上运行。 一种检查方法是在浏览器开发人员工具中打开“网络”选项卡。

我们在这里打开了 Firefox 开发者工具。 您可以在此处突出显示的所选行的文件列中看到@astrojs_turbolinks_client条目。 如果您使用的是 Chrome 开发工具，请打开网络选项卡并查看第一列（名称）。

在这篇文章中，我们研究了如何将 Turbolinks 添加到新的或现有的 Astro 站点。 特别是，我们研究了：

  • 如何使用astro add将 Turbolinks 添加到您的 Astro JS 站点，
  • 如何手动添加和设置 Turbolink 链接到您的 Astro 网站。

如果您正在创建一个新的博客站点，请查看GitHub 上的 Astro Blog Markdown starter ，它使用 Turbolinks。 如果您想查看完整项目中的配置，还可以查看一下。 您也可以在 Stackblitz 中打开项目。

我希望您发现这篇文章很有用，并且很想知道您将如何开始自己的项目以及可能的改进。

你觉得这篇文章有用吗？ 您是否希望看到其他主题的帖子？ 与新帖子的想法取得联系。 另外，如果您喜欢我的写作风格，请与我联系，如果我可以在咨询的基础上为您的公司网站写一些帖子。 请继续阅读以找到联系方式，请参阅下文。 如果您想支持与此类似的帖子并且可以节省几美元、欧元或英镑，请考虑通过给我买杯咖啡来支持我。

最后，请随时在您的社交媒体帐户上为所有觉得有用的关注者分享该帖子。 除了在下面发表评论外，您还可以通过 Twitter 上的@askRodney和Telegram 上的 askRodney联系。 此外，请参阅与 Rodney Lab 取得联系的更多方式。 我定期在Astro和SvelteKit上发帖。 还可以订阅时事通讯以了解我们最新的项目。



原文:

In this post on Astro Turbolinks, we first take a look at what Turbolinks are and why they exist. We then see how you can add Turbolinks to your Astro JS site for fast navigation. You will see it is more or less just a case of adding a package and a couple of lines of config.

When a visitor clicks an internal link on your site (a link to another page on the same site), the browser follows the link and requests the new page. If the site is not too optimised, there might be a flash of white content as the browser waits for the new page. This is known as a full page refresh. Turbolinks offers an alternative model.

⏱ What Turbolinks Does

With Turbolink, when the visitor clicks an internal link, instead of following the link, Turbolinks intercepts it. It then uses something called an XMLHttpRequest to get the page from the server. This is a method which allows the browser to fetch the new page without doing a full page refresh. The smart bit comes when Turbolinks receives the response. It looks for differences between the previous page and the new one. Next, it swaps content which is different on the new page, but keeps identical content. As a final step Turbolinks changes the browser’s url so that it matches the new rendered page.

For users on a slow connection, Turbolinks offers a user experience enhancement. By avoiding the full page refresh, the change will look smoother, allowing the user to carry on with what they were doing in the case only a small part of the page changes.

🚀 Using Turbolinks with Astro

Much of the Turbolinks functionality works automatically behind the scenes. Essentially to add it to your Astro JS site, you just need to install a single package and update config. You can even automate that setup! We will look at a couple of use cases. First, adding Turbolinks to a new Astro site, using automated setup. Then we see how you might add it to an existing Astro project using manual, albeit fairly simple, setup.

🌟 Adding Turbolinks to a New Astro Project 

pnpm create astro@latest my-new-project
cd my-new-project
pnpm install
pnpm astro telemetry disable
pnpm astro add react svelte turbolinks
pnpm run dev

During this setup process you can choose from a list of different site types including Generic and Blog as well as Minimal. It’s not too much bother fully to customise your project later. With that in mind, just choose whichever option sounds closest to what you are doing and take it from there. We disable Astro telemetry here, skip that line if you want to keep it enabled.

In the following line we add Turbolinks. This will prompt you automatically to install the @astrojs/turbolinks package and add configuration to astro.config.mjs. Answer yes to perform these steps automatically. In that same line we are adding setup for Svelte and React. Delete these if you do not need them. Alternatively, introduce others you might want by adding lit, vue, solid or preact to the command.

That’s it! Jump to the test section to check it’s all working.

💫 Adding Turbolinks to an Existing Astro Project

You can also run astro add on an existing project, but might choose to do the setup manually instead. As a first step, install the @astrojs/turbolinks package:

pnpm add -D @astrojs/turbolinks

Then you just need to update astro.config.mjs:

import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config';
import turbolinks from '@astrojs/turbolinks';
import svelte from '@astrojs/svelte';

// https://astro.build/config
export default defineConfig({
  site: 'https://example.com',
  integrations: [svelte(), turbolinks()],
});

Here we import the package we just installed, then add it to any existing integrations.

💯 Astro Turbolinks: Testing it Out

With Astro already being so fast, you might not be able to perceive Turbolinks is working on your dev server. One way to check is to open up the Network tab in your browser developer tools.

We have Firefox developer tools open here. You can see an @astrojs_turbolinks_client entry in the File column of the selected line highlighted here. If you are using Chrome Dev tools, open the Network tab and look in the first (Name) column.

🙌🏽 Astro Turbolinks: Wrapping Up

We have taken a look at adding Turbolinks to a new or existing Astro site in this post. In particular, we looked at:

  • what Turbolinks is and why you might us it,
  • how you can add Turbolinks to your Astro JS site using astro add,
  • how you can manually add and setup Turbolink links to you Astro site.

If you are creating a new blog site, take a look at the Astro Blog Markdown starter on GitHub, which uses Turbolinks. Also take a look if you want to see the configuration in a full project. You can also open the project up in Stackblitz.

I hope you found this article useful and am keen to hear how you will the starter on your own projects as well as possible improvements.

🙏🏽 Astro Turbolinks: Feedback

Have you found the post useful? Would you prefer to see posts on another topic instead? Get in touch with ideas for new posts. Also if you like my writing style, get in touch if I can write some posts for your company site on a consultancy basis. Read on to find ways to get in touch, further below. If you want to support posts similar to this one and can spare a few dollars, euros or pounds, please consider supporting me through Buy me a Coffee.

Finally, feel free to share the post on your social media accounts for all your followers who will find it useful. As well as leaving a comment below, you can get in touch via @askRodney on Twitter and also askRodney on Telegram. Also, see further ways to get in touch with Rodney Lab. I post regularly on Astro as well as SvelteKit. Also subscribe to the newsletter to keep up-to-date with our latest projects.

Items.Title: 插入排序 | Insertion Sort
Items.Description:
我们对插入排序了解多少？

  • 它使用 2 个循环（外部 for 循环和内部 while 循环）和 2 个指针
  • 第一个指针从第二个指针之后的 1 个项目开始
  • 第二个指针从第一个指针之前的 1 个项目开始
  • 第二个循环递减循环直到0
  • 只要当前值大于左侧值，我们就将左侧复制到当前
  • 插入发生在第二个循环完成后
  • 对原始 Array 进行突变
  • 时间复杂度
    • 最佳 -> O(n)
    • 平均 -> O(n^2)
    • 最差 -> O(n^2)
  • 空间复杂度
function InsertionSort ( arr ) { for ( let i = 1 ; i < arr . length ; i ++ ) { let currentValue = arr [ i ]; let oneIndexBefore = i - 1 ; while ( oneIndexBefore >= 0 && arr [ oneIndexBefore ] > currentValue ) { //! SHIFT arr [ oneIndexBefore + 1 ] = arr [ oneIndexBefore ]; //! decrement and look left again oneIndexBefore -- ; } //! INSERT //! after last decrement insert the temp value from before arr [ oneIndexBefore + 1 ] = currentValue ; } return arr ; }
1 Data Structure & Algorithms 2 Bubble Sort 3 Merge Sort 4 Insertion Sort 1数据结构和算法2冒泡排序3合并排序4插入排序

Jan Schenk (he/him) - Jan Schenk (他/他) - May 9 5月9日



原文:

1 Data Structure & Algorithms 2 Bubble Sort 3 Merge Sort 4 Insertion Sort

What do we understand about Insertion Sort?

  • It uses 2 loops (outer for-loop & inner while-loop) and 2 pointers
  • 1st pointer starts from 1 item after the 2nd pointer
  • 2nd pointer starts from 1 item before 1st pointer
  • 2nd loop loops decrementally till 0
  • as long as current value is more than left side value we copy the left side over to current
  • insertion happens after the 2nd loop is finished
  • Mutation is performed on the original Array
  • Time complexity
    • Best -> O(n)
    • Average -> O(n^2)
    • Worst -> O(n^2)
  • Space complexity 
 function InsertionSort(arr) { for (let i = 1; i < arr.length ; i++) { let currentValue = arr[i]; let oneIndexBefore = i - 1; while (oneIndexBefore >= 0 && arr[oneIndexBefore] > currentValue) { //! SHIFT arr[oneIndexBefore + 1] = arr[oneIndexBefore]; //! decrement and look left again oneIndexBefore--; } //! INSERT //! after last decrement insert the temp value from before arr[oneIndexBefore + 1] = currentValue; } return arr; }
1 Data Structure & Algorithms 2 Bubble Sort 3 Merge Sort 4 Insertion Sort

Jan Schenk (he/him) - May 9

Items.Title: 从建造到销售 - EP.07 | From building to selling - EP.07
Items.Description:
欢迎来到 Crossroad 构建之旅的第七天！ 本系列讲述了我从构建到销售的独立黑客产品之旅。 如果您错过了第六天，您可以在这里查看。 如果您想观看重播，我还将今天的Twitch 流上传到YouTube 。

设计销售页面。 上周产品已经完成，是时候回答“人们如何购买这个产品？”。 好吧，为此，您需要在互联网上找到一个人们可以点击“立即购买”按钮的地方！

先说内容。 大多数时候，我很想直接跳入 Figma 并尝试提出设计。 采用这种方法通常会导致花费大量时间使某些东西看起来很漂亮，而您甚至不知道设计是否与您想要讲述的故事相匹配。

所以今天，我没有打开 Figma，而是打开了 Notion 并思考我想在我的登录页面上讲述什么故事。

我想为十字路口带来几个独特的点；

  1. 这是一次性购买产品，因此您无需支付另一项月度服务。 我有兴趣看看我是否可以说服一些 Linktree 用户迁移到 Crossroad。
  2. 生物页面中的链接可以不仅仅是一些社交媒体链接和几个按钮。
  3. 你可以带上你的框架，因为这个产品被分割成块，很容易变成任何框架内的组件。

考虑到这些信息，我开始计算页面的内容。 我想出的是以下结构。

  • 标题，展示一个例子，蝙蝠的权利让人们知道他们可以节省他们的月费。
  • 消除对“这对我来说太难了，我不会编码”的任何疑问。
  • 定价，带有 Gumroad 链接的部分。

我认为页面的流程效果很好。 它率先通过产品，向他们展示设置的容易程度，让他们对 USP 感到兴奋，并询问他们是否想购买该产品。

超越登陆页面。 除了登录页面，我还想设置一个展示所有块的页面。 这样，人们就可以预先知道哪些块是精简版的一部分，哪些是专业版的一部分。

我认为这可以防止混淆，这可能会导致那些对他们获得的区块持怀疑态度的人进行几次销售。 哎呀，有人甚至可能会去阻止页面并获得一些导致销售的灵感。

提供升级路径。 购买精简版的人最终可能希望升级到专业版以访问所有块。 我决定使用 Gumroad 来销售这个产品，其中不包括追加销售，这很糟糕。

为了仍然提供升级路径，我会让人们知道他们可以通过电子邮件向我索取折扣代码。 不是世界上最方便的方式，但总比不提供升级路径要好。

从事设计工作。 我希望着陆页看起来与产品的设计相似。 这意味着使用相同的设计系统/语言。 我不想通过查看登录页面获得的内容与他们通过模板收到的内容之间产生脱节。

当我开始设计时，我对自己说：“我不能这样做；我是一个糟糕的设计师。” 每次我开始一个设计项目时，我都会经历这个阶段。

一天结束时，一旦我开始添加更多图像并稍微修饰间距，我就记住了； 当涉及到设计时，这就是一遍又一遍的迭代。

有时，您只需稍作调整即可获得“这更像是它”的时刻。

只是不停游泳。 更多设计。 明天我会继续做设计。 回顾今天，我们只是在处理了几个线框后做出了一个设计。

我想完成我们今天开始的设计，对其进行多次迭代，一旦完成，我还想对其进行一两个变体。

除了设计之外，我还致力于离线重构。 不用担心; 这只不过是重构 CSS 和重命名块——有意义的工作，但在流上分享有点无聊。

再次感谢您阅读此构建日志。 祝你有美好的一天，我明天见（或今天在我的直播中）！ 如果您想同时保持更新，请在 Twitter 上关注我。

带着爱，

旺克鲁伊



原文:

Welcome to day seven of the Crossroad build journey! This series follows my journey of indie hacking a product from building to selling. If you missed day six, you could check it out here. I also uploaded today's Twitch stream to YouTube if you want to watch the replay.

Designing the sales page. 💰

With the product as good as finished last week, it’s time to answer “how can people purchase this product?”. Well, for that, you need a place on the internet where people can hit a “buy now” button!

Content first. ✍️

Most of the time, I am tempted to jump straight into Figma and try to come up with a design. Taking this approach usually results in spending a lot of time making something look pretty while you don’t even know if the design matches the story you want to tell.

So today, instead of opening Figma, I opened up Notion and thought about what story I wanted to tell on my landing page.

I want to bring across a couple of unique points for crossroads;

  1. It’s a one-time purchase product, so you aren’t paying for another monthly service. I am interested in seeing if I can persuade some Linktree users to migrate to Crossroad.
  2. A link in bio page can be more than just some social media links and a couple of buttons.
  3. You can bring your framework, as this product is split up into blocks that are easy to turn into components inside any framework.

With this information in mind, I started to work out the content for the page. What I came up with is the following structure.

  • Header, show an example and right of the bat let people know they can save on their monthly fees.
  • • Video, show the lead that even if they can’t code, it only takes a couple of minutes to set up your page. To take away any doubts about “this is too difficult for me, I can’t code.”
  • USPs, going more in-depth about how Crossroads differs from other links in bio templates and services.
  • Pricing, section with links to Gumroad.

I think the flow of the page works pretty well. It takes the lead through the product, shows them how easy it is to set up, gets them excited with the USPs, and asks them if they want to buy the product.

Beyond the landing page. 🧑‍🚀

Besides the landing page, I also want to set up a page that showcases all the blocks. This way, people know upfront what blocks are part of the lite version and which are part of the pro version.

I think this can prevent confusion, which might lead to a couple of sales for people who are otherwise doubtful about what blocks they are otherwise getting. Heck, someone might even go to the block page and gets some inspiration which leads to a sale.

Providing an upgrade path. 📈

People who purchase the lite version might eventually like to upgrade to the Pro version to get access to all the blocks. I have decided to use Gumroad for selling this product, which doesn’t include upselling, which sucks.

To still offer an upgrade path, I will let people know that they can email me for a discount code. Not the most convenient way in the world, but better than not providing an upgrade path.

Working on the designs. 🎨

I want the landing page to look similar to the product's design. That means using the same design system/language. I don't want to create a disconnect between what people are expecting by looking at the landing page to get and what they receive with the template.

When I started working on the designs, I thought to myself, "I can't do this; I am a horrible designer." I always go through this phase every time I start a design project.

At the end of the day, once I started adding more images and polished the spacing a bit, I remembered; that it's all about iterating over and over when it comes to designs.

Sometimes you are just a couple of tweaks away from having a "this is more like it" moment.

Just keep swimming. 🐟

More designs. ✏️

Tomorrow I’ll continue working on the designs. Looking back on today, we merely made one design after working on a couple of wireframes.

I want to finish the design that we started with today, iterate over it a couple of times, and once that’s done, I also want to make one or two variations of it.

Besides the design, I am also working on the refactor off stream. Don’t worry; it’s nothing more than refactoring the CSS and renaming the blocks—meaningful work but a bit boring to share on stream.

Thanks again for reading this build log. Have a great day, and I’ll see you tomorrow (or in my stream today)! 🚀

If you want to stay updated in the meantime, give me a follow on Twitter.

With love,

Twankrui

Items.Author: anoshalassi

Items.Title: 处理巨大的拉取请求 | Tackling huge pull requests
Items.Description:
更大的任务需要大量的代码更改，有时会导致巨大的拉取请求，几乎不可能正确审查。

我们怎样才能减轻这种情况？ 首先想到的是不要让它发生在首位。 如何？ 通过将任务分解为更小的任务并逐步实施。 虽然更可取，但这种方法并不总是可行的。

一个典型的例子是对到处使用的框架或库的重大升级。 如果升级碰巧引入了许多重大更改，则上述方法将变得毫无用处。

这种情况的解决方案是以更容易审查的方式构建提交。 例如，GitHub 提供了一项功能，允许审阅者逐个提交审阅拉取请求提交。

起初这听起来很麻烦，因为除了实现实际任务之外，您还必须牢记提交更改的方式。 不要随意提交代码，或者更糟糕的是——在完成之前根本不提交，你必须深思熟虑地做出提交。

但如果从正确的角度来看，这实际上是一个优势。 虽然这种方法的要求肯定更高，但它的优势在于迫使您制定一个高层次的计划，以深思熟虑地处理您的任务。 这迫使您将您的实施视为您需要对解决方案采取的一系列步骤。 这可以防止您刚开始破解并在之后发现自己有一堆不相关的更改文件，很难提出提交消息。

你甚至可以更进一步。 您可能会同意，格式更改可能会混淆为完成手头任务而进行的实际更改。 为了减轻这种情况，我们可以让自己在单独的提交中提交格式更改。 如果我们将这些提交标记为“仅格式化更改”，审阅者可以安全地忽略它们，我们将进一步简化审阅过程。

请记住，最终必须有人审查您正在编写的代码并尝试减轻审查者的工作。 审稿人是你的朋友 :)



原文:

Bigger tasks require a lot of code changes, sometimes resulting in huge pull requests which are nearly impossible to review properly.

How can we mitigate that? The first thing which comes to mind is to not let it happen in the first place. How? By diving the task into smaller tasks and implement them step by step. Although preferable, this approach is not always possible.

A prime example for that is a major upgrade of a framework or a library which used all over the place. In case that upgrade happens to introduce a lot of breaking changes, it renders the aforementioned approach useless.

A solution for such a case is to structure your commits in a way that makes it easier to review. GitHub, for example, provides a feature which allows the reviewer to review the pull request commit by commit.

At first it sounds cumbersome, because in addition to the implementing the actual task you also have to keep in mind the way in which you commit your changes. Instead of committing you code at random, or event worse - not committing at all until your finished, you have to make your commits thoughtfully.

But this is actually an advantage if you look at it from the right angle. While this approach is definitely more demanding it has the advantage of forcing you to create a high level plan, to approach you task thoughtfully. This forces you to think of your implementation as a sequence of steps which you need to take to the solution. This prevents you to just start hacking away and finding yourself afterwards with a bunch of unrelated changed files, having a hard time coming up with a commit message.

You can even go step a further. You will probably agree that formatting changes can obfuscate the real changes that were made in order to accomplish the task at hand. In order to mitigate that we can make ourselves commit changes in formatting in separate commit. If we mark these commits as "formatting changes only" they can safely be ignored by the reviewer, and we will further ease the reviewing process.

Keep in mind that someone will eventually have to review the code you're writing and try to ease the work of the reviewer. The reviewer is you friend :)
Items.Author: senjinhajrulahovic

Items.Title: 清洁代码提示：并非所有评论都是坏的 | Clean Code Tip: Not all comments are bad
Items.Description:
很多开发者说

所有评论都不好！ 错误的！ 大部分评论都不好！

差评举例

比如看这个方法，看评论：

 /// <summary> Checks if the password is valid </summary> /// <param name="password">The password to be validated</param> /// <returns>True if the password is valid, false otherwise</returns> public bool IsPasswordValid ( string password ) { Regex regex = new Regex ( @"[az]{2,7}[1-9]{3,4}" ); var hasMatch = regex . IsMatch ( password ); return hasMatch ; }

这里的注释毫无意义——它们只是告诉您可以通过查看方法签名推断出的相同内容：此方法检查输入字符串是否为有效密码。

所以，是的，这些评论完全没有意义，应该避免。

好的评论类型

但是，在某些情况下，写评论很有帮助。

 public bool IsPasswordValid ( string password ) { // 2 to 7 lowercase chars followed by 3 or 4 numbers // Valid: kejix173 // aoe193 // Invalid: a92881 Regex regex = new Regex ( @"[az]{2,7}[1-9]{3,4}" ); return regex . IsMatch ( password ); }

此处注释的目的不是解释该方法的作用（它已经非常明确），而是通过示例解释用于验证密码的正则表达式。 另一种解释它的方法是添加验证某些输入字符串的测试。 通过这种方式，您可以确保文档（也称为测试）始终与生产代码保持一致。

顺便说一句，对于更复杂的计算，添加注释解释为什么（而不是如何或做什么）一段代码是帮助开发人员理解代码的好方法。

添加注释的另一个原因是解释为什么存在特定的代码：示例是法律法规、相关工作项或对您找到该特定解决方案的位置的引用。

结论

写注释时要始终注意：是的，它们通常只是把代码弄得乱七八糟。 但在某些情况下，它们确实可以为代码增加价值。

要阅读有关好评和差评的更多信息，您可能会喜欢以下详细的文章：

快乐编码！

🐧 🐧



原文:

Many developers say that

All comments are bad! 💢

False! Most of the comments are bad!

Examples of bad comments

For example, look at this method, and look at the comments:

/// <summary> Checks if the password is valid </summary>
/// <param name="password">The password to be validated</param>
/// <returns>True if the password is valid, false otherwise</returns>
public bool IsPasswordValid(string password)
{ Regex regex = new Regex(@"[a-z]{2,7}[1-9]{3,4}"); var hasMatch = regex.IsMatch(password); return hasMatch;
}

Here the comments are pointless - they just tell the same things you can infer by looking at the method signature: this method checks if the input string is a valid password.

So, yes, those kinds of comments are totally meaningless, and they should be avoided.

Good types of comments

But still, there are cases when writing comments is pretty helpful.

public bool IsPasswordValid(string password)
{ // 2 to 7 lowercase chars followed by 3 or 4 numbers // Valid: kejix173 // aoe193 // Invalid: a92881 Regex regex = new Regex(@"[a-z]{2,7}[1-9]{3,4}"); return regex.IsMatch(password);
}

Here the purpose of the comment is not to explain what the method does (it's already pretty explicit), but it explains with examples the Regular Expression used to validate the password. Another way to explain it is by adding tests that validate some input strings. In this way, you make sure that the documentation (aka the tests) is always aligned with the production code.

By the way, for more complex calculations, adding comments explaining WHY (and not HOW or WHAT) a piece of code does is a good way to help developers understand the code.

Another reason to add comments is to explain why a specific piece of code exists: examples are legal regulations, related work items, or references to where you've found that particular solution.

Conclusion

Always pay attention when writing comments: yes, they often just clutter the code. But they can really add value to the code, in some cases.

To read more about good and bad comments, here's a well-detailed article you might like:

🔗 Clean code tips - comments and formatting

Happy coding!

🐧
